title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Install required Python packages for statistical analysis, rule extraction, and benchmarking. Set up virtual environment and verify all dependencies.

steps.step1: Create and activate Python virtual environment: python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install core dependencies: pip install numpy scipy pandas scikit-learn scikit-learn-intelex wurlitzer
steps.step3: Install rule extraction: pip install wittgenstein ripper sklearn-rules
steps.step4: Install benchmarking tools: pip install psutil memory_profiler
steps.step5: Install visualization: pip install matplotlib seaborn
steps.step6: Verify installation by importing all packages: python -c 'import numpy, scipy, pandas, sklearn, wittgenstein, psutil, matplotlib'
steps.step7: Set random seed for reproducibility: export KAPHE_SEED=42

title: Configure Kernel Simulation Framework
description: Set up the simulation framework that models kernel subsystem behavior and workload interactions. This avoids the need for actual kernel modifications while still providing valid performance characteristics.

steps.step1: Create directory structure: mkdir -p experiments/{data,models,results,figures}
steps.step2: Implement kernel configuration space definition with 12 tunable parameters across memory (vm.swappiness, vm.dirty_ratio, vm.dirty_background_ratio, vm.vfs_cache_pressure), I/O (scheduler_type, nr_requests, read_ahead_kb), and scheduling (sched_latency_ns, sched_min_granularity_ns, sched_migration_cost_ns)
steps.step3: Define configuration constraints: swappiness ∈ [0,100], dirty_ratio ∈ [1,50], dirty_background_ratio ∈ [1,20], vfs_cache_pressure ∈ [1,1000], scheduler_type ∈ {none, mq-deadline, bfq}, nr_requests ∈ [32,1024], read_ahead_kb ∈ [0,2048], sched_latency_ns ∈ [1000000,10000000], sched_min_granularity_ns ∈ [100000,1000000], sched_migration_cost_ns ∈ [100000,500000]
steps.step4: Implement workload-to-performance simulator based on published kernel behavior models from Matias et al. (2015) and Lawall et al. (2024)
steps.step5: Validate simulator output against known kernel tuning guidelines (e.g., low swappiness for databases, bfq for desktop workloads)

title: Generate Synthetic Workload Signatures
description: Create a diverse set of synthetic workload signatures representing different application types. Each signature captures workload characteristics that correlate with kernel configuration sensitivity.

steps.step1: Define workload feature space with 8 dimensions: alloc_rate (allocations/sec/thread), working_set_MB, thread_count, thread_churn_per_sec, io_read_MBps, io_write_MBps, io_sequentiality_ratio, syscall_rate_per_sec
steps.step2: Generate 240 training workloads (60 per category): In-Memory DB (high alloc_rate, small working_set), Analytics (medium alloc_rate, large working_set, high io_sequentiality), Web Services (medium alloc_rate, connection churn), Build/Compile (fork-heavy, bursty I/O)
steps.step3: Generate 48 test workloads (12 per category) using different random seeds (seeds: 123, 456, 789)
steps.step4: Generate 16 real validation workloads (4 per category) for actual kernel testing
steps.step5: Save workload signatures to experiments/data/workloads_train.csv, experiments/data/workloads_test.csv, experiments/data/workloads_real.csv
steps.step6: Compute and log workload statistics: mean, std, min, max for each feature per category

title: Collect Profiling Data via Simulation
description: Run all workloads under different kernel configurations to build the training dataset. This simulates the profiling phase where eBPF would collect metrics.

steps.step1: Define 20 configuration combinations spanning the configuration space: 5 memory configs × 2 I/O scheduler types × 2 scheduling configs
steps.step2: For each of 240 training workloads, simulate execution under all 20 configurations
steps.step3: Record performance metrics: throughput (ops/sec), latency_p50 (ms), latency_p99 (ms)
steps.step4: Calculate normalized performance score: score = throughput / (1 + latency_p50/1000)
steps.step5: Identify optimal configuration per workload (oracle)
steps.step6: Save profiling dataset to experiments/data/profiling_results.csv with columns: workload_id, category, workload_features..., config_id, config_params..., throughput, latency_p50, latency_p99, score, is_optimal
steps.step7: Compute ground truth sensitivity: which workload features correlate with configuration sensitivity using Pearson correlation

title: Baseline: Default Kernel Configuration
description: Evaluate performance using default Linux kernel configurations as the simplest baseline. This represents what most systems use without tuning.

steps.step1: Define default configuration: swappiness=60, dirty_ratio=20, dirty_background_ratio=10, vfs_cache_pressure=100, scheduler_type=mq-deadline, nr_requests=128, read_ahead_kb=128, sched_latency_ns=6000000, sched_min_granularity_ns=400000, sched_migration_cost_ns=250000
steps.step2: Run all 288 workloads (240 train + 48 test) under default config using simulator
steps.step3: Calculate performance metrics: normalized_score = score_default / score_oracle
steps.step4: Record results: experiments/results/baseline_default.csv
steps.step5: Compute aggregate statistics: mean normalized score, % of workloads within 10% of optimal

title: Baseline: Expert Heuristics
description: Implement expert-derived rules from Linux tuning guides (Red Hat Performance Tuning Guide, Ubuntu Kernel Tuning documentation). These represent current best practices.

steps.step1: Extract rules from Red Hat Enterprise Linux Performance Tuning Guide and Ubuntu Kernel Tuning documentation:
steps.step2: Rule 1 (Database): IF alloc_rate > 500 AND working_set < 1000 THEN swappiness=10, dirty_ratio=5, scheduler_type=none
steps.step3: Rule 2 (Analytics): IF working_set > 10000 AND io_sequentiality_ratio > 0.8 THEN swappiness=10, read_ahead_kb=1024, vfs_cache_pressure=50
steps.step4: Rule 3 (Web Server): IF syscall_rate_per_sec > 10000 THEN scheduler_type=mq-deadline, sched_latency_ns=3000000
steps.step5: Rule 4 (Build): IF thread_churn_per_sec > 100 THEN dirty_background_ratio=5, sched_min_granularity_ns=200000
steps.step6: Apply rules to all 288 workloads, using default config if no rule matches
steps.step8: Compute coverage: % of workloads matching at least one expert rule

title: Baseline: MLKAPS-Style Decision Tree
description: Implement a decision tree classifier trained on the same profiling data, representing the MLKAPS approach but applied to OS kernel tuning. This is our strong baseline.

steps.step1: Prepare training data: features = workload features, labels = optimal configuration ID (1-20)
steps.step2: Train DecisionTreeClassifier from sklearn with hyperparameters: max_depth=10, min_samples_leaf=5, random_state=42
steps.step3: Train GradientBoostingClassifier as ensemble alternative: n_estimators=50, max_depth=5, random_state=42
steps.step4: Evaluate on test set (48 workloads): predict configuration, simulate performance
steps.step5: Compute accuracy: % of correct optimal predictions
steps.step7: Extract interpretability metrics: tree depth, number of nodes, number of leaves
steps.step8: Export decision tree rules using sklearn.tree.export_text for comparison

title: KAPHE Phase 1: Statistical Characterization
description: Implement the characterization phase of KAPHE using statistical analysis to identify which workload features predict configuration sensitivity.

steps.step1: For each configuration parameter, compute effect size (Cohen's d) between top-5 and bottom-5 performing workloads
steps.step2: Compute Pearson correlation between each workload feature and performance improvement over default
steps.step3: Perform ANOVA for categorical configs (scheduler_type) vs performance
steps.step4: Compute two-way ANOVA for interaction effects between memory and I/O configurations
steps.step5: Generate sensitivity matrix: rows = workload features, cols = config parameters, values = correlation strength
steps.step7: Select top-k most predictive features for each configuration parameter
steps.step9: Generate heatmap visualization of sensitivity matrix

title: KAPHE Phase 2: Rule Extraction with RIPPER
description: Apply the RIPPER rule induction algorithm to extract interpretable IF-THEN rules mapping workload characteristics to optimal configurations.

steps.step1: Preprocess data: binarize optimal config labels (one-vs-rest for each of 20 configs)
steps.step2: Apply RIPPER (wurlitzer/wittgenstein library) with parameters: max_rules=30, max_rule_conds=5, max_total_conds=150, k=2 (optimization runs)
steps.step3: Extract rules for each configuration: IF feature_conditions THEN recommend_config
steps.step4: Compute rule quality metrics:
steps.step5: - Confidence: precision on training data (#correct / #triggered)
steps.step6: - Coverage: % of training workloads triggering each rule
steps.step7: - Expected improvement: mean performance gain when rule fires
steps.step8: - Rule length: number of conditions per rule
steps.step9: Filter rules: keep only those with confidence >= 0.7 and coverage >= 0.05

title: KAPHE Phase 3: Recommendation Engine Evaluation
description: Evaluate KAPHE's recommendation engine on held-out test workloads using the extracted rules.

steps.step1: For each of 48 test workloads:
steps.step2: a. Match workload features against extracted rules (order by confidence, break ties by coverage)
steps.step3: b. If multiple rules match, select highest confidence; if no rules match, use default config
steps.step4: c. Simulate performance with recommended configuration
steps.step5: Compute metrics:
steps.step6: - Recommendation accuracy: % of workloads within 5% of oracle performance
steps.step9: - Rule coverage on test set: % of workloads matching at least one rule
steps.step10: - Lookup time: measure rule matching latency (should be <1ms)
steps.step12: Run with 3 random seeds (42, 123, 456) for rule extraction, report mean and std

title: Ablation: Without Statistical Characterization
description: Ablation study to measure the impact of the characterization phase. Train RIPPER directly on all features without feature selection.

steps.step1: Run RIPPER on full 8-dimensional feature space without pre-selection
steps.step2: Compare against KAPHE with feature selection
steps.step3: Measure: rule count, average rule length, accuracy, coverage
steps.step4: Hypothesis: Without characterization, rules will be more complex (more conditions) with lower interpretability
steps.step5: Record results: experiments/results/ablation_no_characterization.csv

title: Ablation: Without Rule Induction (Direct Mapping)
description: Ablation study using nearest-neighbor matching instead of rule induction. Maps workloads to nearest training example and uses its optimal config.

steps.step1: Implement k-NN baseline: k=5, features normalized
steps.step2: For test workload, find 5 nearest neighbors in training set
steps.step3: Use majority vote of their optimal configurations
steps.step4: Measure accuracy and performance vs. KAPHE rules
steps.step5: Hypothesis: k-NN will have competitive accuracy but zero interpretability (no rules to inspect)

title: Ablation: Effect of Training Set Size
description: Study how KAPHE performance scales with amount of profiling data.

steps.step1: Subsample training data: [30, 60, 120, 240] workloads (reduced points for time)
steps.step2: For each size, extract rules and evaluate on full test set (48 workloads)
steps.step3: Measure: rule count, accuracy, coverage as function of training size
steps.step4: Plot learning curve: accuracy vs. training set size
steps.step5: Record results: experiments/results/ablation_scaling.csv

title: Interpretability Analysis: Quantitative Metrics
description: Compute established interpretability metrics for KAPHE rules and compare against MLKAPS decision tree baseline.

steps.step1: For KAPHE rules, compute:
steps.step2: - Rule complexity: total_rules + avg_conditions_per_rule
steps.step3: - Rule coverage: % of test cases covered by extracted rules
steps.step4: - Fidelity: agreement between rule recommendations and oracle on test set (%)
steps.step5: - Average rule length: mean number of conditions
steps.step6: - Maximum rule length: longest rule in conditions
steps.step8: - Tree complexity: number of nodes + tree depth
steps.step9: - Number of decision paths (equivalent to rules)
steps.step10: - Average path length from root to leaf

title: Real Kernel Validation Experiment
description: Validate simulation results on actual Linux kernel with sysctl-tunable parameters. Limited to 2 representative workloads for feasibility within time budget.

steps.step1: Select 2 validation workloads: Redis (in-mem DB) and Nginx (web server)
steps.step2: For each workload, define concrete benchmark:
steps.step3: - Redis: redis-benchmark -t set,get -n 100000 -c 50 (30s duration)
steps.step4: - Nginx: wrk -t4 -c100 -d30s http://localhost/
steps.step5: Run workload under default configuration, record baseline performance
steps.step6: Apply KAPHE-recommended configuration based on workload profiling:
steps.step7: - Profile workload for 20 seconds using /proc/stat, /proc/meminfo, iostat
steps.step11: Measure: actual performance improvement, validate prediction accuracy
steps.step12: Compare against simulation prediction for same workload signature

title: Overhead Measurement
description: Measure the runtime overhead of KAPHE profiling and recommendation phases.

steps.step1: Profiling overhead: simulate eBPF collection cost (known to be <2% from Craun et al., 2024)
steps.step2: Recommendation latency: time rule matching for 1000 random workloads
steps.step3: Compute: mean, p50, p99 lookup time
steps.step4: Target: profiling overhead <3%, recommendation latency <1ms
steps.step5: Record: experiments/results/overhead_metrics.csv

title: Statistical Significance Testing
description: Perform statistical tests to validate that KAPHE improvements are significant and not due to chance.

steps.step1: Paired t-test: KAPHE vs Default (null hypothesis: no difference)
steps.step2: Paired t-test: KAPHE vs Expert Heuristics
steps.step3: Paired t-test: KAPHE vs MLKAPS Decision Tree
steps.step4: Report p-values for all tests
steps.step5: Effect size: Cohen's d for KAPHE vs each baseline

title: Cross-Workload Generalization Test
description: Test whether rules extracted from one workload category generalize to others.

steps.step1: Train KAPHE rules on single category only (e.g., In-Memory DB)
steps.step2: Test on all 4 categories
steps.step3: Measure accuracy within category vs. across categories
steps.step4: Repeat for each category as training set
steps.step5: Compute generalization matrix: rows = training category, cols = test category
steps.step6: Hypothesis: Within-category accuracy > 70%, cross-category lower but still reasonable

title: Generate Paper Figures
description: Create publication-quality figures for the research paper.

steps.step1: Figure 1: Overview diagram - workflow of KAPHE three phases
steps.step2: Figure 2: Sensitivity heatmap - correlation between workload features and config parameters
steps.step3: Figure 3: Performance comparison bar chart - KAPHE vs baselines with error bars
steps.step4: Figure 4: Rule examples - showcase extracted rules with confidence/coverage
steps.step5: Figure 5: Ablation study results
steps.step6: Figure 6: Interpretability comparison - radar chart KAPHE vs MLKAPS
steps.step7: Save all figures to experiments/figures/ in PDF and PNG formats

title: Generate Results Tables
description: Create LaTeX-formatted tables for the paper.

steps.step1: Table 1: Main results - performance metrics for all methods
steps.step2: Table 2: Interpretability metrics comparison
steps.step3: Table 3: Extracted rules summary (top 10 rules)
steps.step4: Table 4: Ablation study results
steps.step5: Table 5: Statistical significance test results
steps.step6: Save tables as .tex files in experiments/figures/

title: Final Summary and Success Criteria Check
description: Evaluate all success criteria and determine if the research succeeded.

steps.step1: Check Technical Criterion: KAPHE shows statistically significant improvement over default (p < 0.05)?
steps.step2: Check Generalization Criterion: Within 20% degradation when generalizing across categories?
steps.step3: Check Practicality Criterion: Profiling overhead < 3% and recommendation latency < 1ms?
steps.step4: Check Interpretability Criterion: Avg rule length ≤ 5, total rules ≤ 30, fidelity ≥ 80%?
steps.step5: Generate summary report: experiments/results/success_criteria_check.md
steps.step6: If criteria met, research succeeds; else document failure conditions encountered
